topic: Sharp efficiency-equivalence/non-equivalence theorem across the four leading staggered DiD estimators: Borusyak-Jaravel-Spiess (2024 RES) imputation, Sun-Abraham (2021 JoE) interaction-weighted, Callaway-Sant'Anna (2021 JoE) doubly-robust group-time, and Wooldridge (2021) cohort-time-saturated TWFE. Under heterogeneous treatment effects in cohort and event-time, all four point-identify the same average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) under no-anticipation + parallel trends. The flagship question (axis b, equivalence/non-equivalence frontier between named published estimators): characterize the sharp boundary at which these four estimators have asymptotically equivalent influence functions vs. strictly different ones, as a function of (i) the cohort-time treatment-effect heterogeneity matrix Tau, (ii) the cohort-share vector pi, and (iii) the pre-period covariance matrix Sigma. The kernel claim is a closed-form spectral non-equivalence theorem: the BJS imputation IF and the SA-CS-Wooldridge IFs span identical orthogonal-score subspaces if and only if a low-rank condition rank(Tau) <= K_star holds, where K_star is a sharp threshold equal to the smallest singular value of an explicit observable kernel matrix combining pi and Sigma; when rank(Tau) > K_star, the four estimators have strictly distinct asymptotic variances and BJS achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound while the others sacrifice efficiency proportional to the rank gap. K_star is a new mathematical object computable from observable cohort shares and pre-period covariances, not an existence/absence statement from prior work. Recovers the well-known equivalence under homogeneous effects (rank(Tau)=0) and provides the first sharp efficiency-equivalence boundary for staggered DiD in closed form.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize necessary and sufficient observable conditions under which BJS imputation, Sun-Abraham interaction-weighted, Callaway-Sant'Anna group-time, and Wooldridge saturated-TWFE estimators have the same first-order influence-function subspace for a fixed staggered-adoption ATT aggregation.

source_refs.claim: Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess derive an efficient imputation estimator for staggered event-study designs with unrestricted treatment-effect heterogeneity, but do not give a four-estimator IF-equivalence frontier.
source_refs.author: Borusyak, Jaravel, Spiess

source_refs.claim: Sun and Abraham show event-study TWFE coefficients can be contaminated by other periods under heterogeneous effects and propose interaction-weighted estimators, leaving efficiency comparison to other robust estimators unresolved.
source_refs.author: Sun, Abraham

source_refs.claim: Callaway and Sant'Anna identify and aggregate group-time average treatment effects in multi-period DiD, but the paper does not characterize equality of their estimator's IF with BJS or saturated TWFE implementations.
source_refs.author: Callaway, Sant'Anna

source_refs.claim: Wooldridge establishes TWFE/Mundlak/pooled-OLS equivalences for intervention analysis with staggered entry and heterogeneous effects, but not a semiparametric IF-equivalence boundary with BJS, SA, and CS.
source_refs.author: Wooldridge

source_refs.excerpt: flagship rank-phase and discontinuity statements are Conjectures, not proved theorems
generic open-set theorem over aggregation matrices and cohort smoothness operators

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published estimators solve adjacent pieces of the staggered-DiD problem: BJS gives an efficient imputation route, SA removes event-study contamination, CS targets group-time ATTs and aggregations, and Wooldridge gives saturated-regression implementation equivalences. None supplies a single tangent-space or IF-subspace comparison across all four named estimators for the same ATT target. The closest in-repo analogue is a cohort-vs-aggregate row-space frontier, and its reviewers explicitly left the operator certificate conjectural rather than proved.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving necessary and sufficient rank, null-space, or spectral conditions on the ATT aggregation map, cohort shares, pre-period covariance, and heterogeneity matrix under which the four estimators' influence functions are equal, plus a strict-variance separation statement off that set.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine when multiple pre-treatment periods and comparison groups change the efficient influence function for staggered DiD/event-study ATTs rather than only improving diagnostics or finite-sample precision.

source_refs.claim: Chen, Sant'Anna, and Xie show modern DiD assumptions imply nonparametric overidentification restrictions and derive closed-form EIFs for DiD and event-study parameters.
source_refs.author: Chen, Sant'Anna, Xie

source_refs.claim: Sant'Anna and Zhao derive semiparametric efficiency bounds for ATT in two-period DiD designs and show their doubly robust estimators can attain them under correct working models.
source_refs.author: Sant'Anna, Zhao

source_refs.claim: BJS motivate efficient imputation by exploiting pre-treatment observations in staggered event-study designs with heterogeneous treatment effects.
source_refs.author: Borusyak, Jaravel, Spiess

source_refs.excerpt: normalized boundary limits s_j(K), and the active nuisance subspace
singular values of the event-window FWL Gram block

open_problems.why_unsolved: The efficiency literature now states that extra pre-period moment restrictions can matter for EIF construction, while older event-study work often treats pre-periods as contamination checks or design diagnostics. The missing boundary is the finite-dimensional condition that says whether extra pre-period covariance directions enter the efficient score for a particular ATT aggregation, are redundant, or are lost in a near-null FWL direction. The in-repo spectral proposal flags exactly this kind of retained-row-space boundary but does not connect it to semiparametric EIFs.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A projection theorem for the EIF onto the span of sequential DiD moment restrictions, with an explicit singular-value or row-space condition separating score-changing pre-period information from redundant diagnostics.

open_problems.open_problem: Give a generic computable operator certificate for when an aggregate staggered-DiD sensitivity frontier is the exact projection of the cohort-period frontier rather than conservative or anti-conservative.

source_refs.excerpt: interpretation as sharp cohort-level breakdown frontiers requires an additional quotient-rank condition
explicit computable certificate that is not tautological from h_agg/h_coh

open_problems.why_unsolved: Prior ThmSmith work isolated the right finite-dimensional objects, but the hard step was not the LP-dual threshold itself; it was proving an ex ante operator test that classifies sharp, conservative, and anti-conservative regions before solving both support-function programs. The reviewer specifically rejected a ratio diagnostic as insufficient and requested a generic open-set theorem over aggregation matrices and smoothness operators.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An active-face row-space theorem that outputs a basis-invariant matrix certificate for equality, conservativeness, and anti-conservativeness of the aggregate and cohort-level support frontiers.

open_problems.open_problem: Decide whether covariance-whitened singular-value thresholds for event-study breakdown points remain valid econometric frontiers outside the quadratic Honest-DiD proxy.

source_refs.excerpt: thresholds are singular-value functionals of an observable kernel matrix
kernel conjecture is a sharp three-regime phase transition
state the general threshold through the standard trust-region secular equation

open_problems.why_unsolved: The rejected angle had a closed-form spectral flavor close to the requested K-star idea, but reviewers judged the result too close to known trust-region geometry unless it was connected to a genuine econometric sensitivity or inference frontier. The proposal's own scope flags also say the active-face certificate is provisional outside the quadratic proxy, so the missing issue is whether the spectral threshold survives in the actual polyhedral or support-function problem.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem or counterexample showing that the observable covariance-whitened singular-value threshold is equivalent to the true event-study support frontier, or fails in a named non-quadratic regime.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when modern group-time ATT and event-study estimators that are point-identification-equivalent differ only by target aggregation weights versus by first-order asymptotic variance.

source_refs.claim: Callaway and Sant'Anna separate group-time ATT identification from aggregation and inference in DiD with multiple periods and variation in treatment timing.
source_refs.author: Callaway, Sant'Anna

source_refs.claim: Sun and Abraham show nominal event-time coefficients can load on the wrong cohort-specific effects, motivating interaction-weighted estimands.
source_refs.author: Sun, Abraham

source_refs.claim: Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe synthesize the recent DiD literature around assumptions, estimands, and implementation choices, highlighting unresolved estimator-choice issues.
source_refs.author: Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, Poe

open_problems.why_unsolved: The literature has strong results on contamination and interpretable aggregation, but those are not the same as an IF comparison. Two estimators can target the same written ATT aggregation while using different nuisance projections, comparison sets, or estimated weights, and current papers do not give a clean algebraic condition under which those differences vanish at first order.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient theorem equating the estimator-specific aggregation and nuisance-projection operators modulo the ATT target's null space, with a variance-gap formula when equality fails.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the efficiency frontier for staggered-DiD ATT estimators in unbalanced panels where available pre-period covariance information and comparison sets vary by cohort.

source_refs.claim: Belle'go, Benatia, and Dortet-Bernadet develop chained DiD for unbalanced staggered panels and discuss identification and efficiency gains relative to using subsets of the data.
source_refs.author: Belle'go, Benatia, Dortet-Bernadet

source_refs.claim: BJS derive an efficient imputation estimator in the balanced staggered-event-study framework, but do not settle the full unbalanced-panel covariance frontier.
source_refs.author: Borusyak, Jaravel, Spiess

open_problems.why_unsolved: Chained DiD shows that overlapping incomplete panels can deliver efficiency gains, while BJS gives an efficient imputation benchmark for standard staggered event studies. What remains missing is a single frontier that tells when cohort-specific missingness changes the efficient score, when it merely changes weights, and when a BJS-style imputation, CS-style group-time aggregation, or chained estimator exhausts the usable covariance information.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp EIF or semiparametric variance-bound formula indexed by the cohort-time missingness pattern, with equality conditions comparing imputation, group-time, and chained aggregation estimators.

literature_map:
Borusyak-Jaravel-Spiess (2024, RES) is the efficiency anchor: staggered event-study DiD with heterogeneous causal effects and an efficient imputation estimator.
Sun-Abraham (2021, JoE) is the contamination anchor: lead/lag TWFE coefficients can load on other-period effects, and interaction-weighted estimators remove that contamination.
Callaway-Sant'Anna (2021, JoE) is the group-time ATT anchor: identify ATT(g,t), then aggregate and conduct inference.
Wooldridge (2021, SSRN) supplies the saturated TWFE/Mundlak equivalence implementation route for staggered entry and heterogeneous effects.
Goodman-Bacon (2021, JoE) and de Chaisemartin-D'Haultfoeuille (2020, AER) explain why ordinary TWFE weights can be nonconvex or negative under treatment-effect heterogeneity.
Sant'Anna-Zhao (2020, JoE) gives two-period doubly robust DiD efficiency bounds, which are a natural lower-dimensional efficiency comparator.
Chen-Sant'Anna-Xie (2025, arXiv/Cowles) derives closed-form EIFs for modern DiD/event-study parameters and emphasizes overidentification restrictions from multiple pre-periods.
Roth-Sant'Anna-Bilinski-Poe (2023, JoE) synthesize the modern DiD literature and foreground unresolved estimator-choice and implementation issues.
Belle'go-Benatia-Dortet-Bernadet (2025, JoE) extends efficiency questions to unbalanced staggered panels through chained DiD.
The live gap across these papers is not point identification of ATT, but exact first-order equivalence: when named robust estimators have the same influence function, and when their distinct nuisance projections produce a strict variance gap.
prior_proposal_map:
No active proposal tex exists yet for eid_bjs_efficient_event_study; the active directory only has state and pipeline JSON.
Closest DiD sources mined were panel_honest_did_phase, pid_did_anticipation_bounded, q1_spectral_phase_transition, and legacy q1_partial_did_unobserved_trends.
panel_honest_did_phase is the strongest internal analogue to a K-star frontier: it has quotient row-space, active-face, covariance, and singular-value language for cohort-vs-aggregate staggered DiD sensitivity.
Its reviewer JSONs yielded high-signal strengthening_paths asking for a generic open-set theorem and an explicit computable operator certificate.
The angle0 rejected proposal is a warning: a bare singular-value threshold can be dismissed as trust-region geometry unless tied to a genuine econometric estimator or inference frontier.
q1_spectral_phase_transition gives a rank/row-space phase-transition template for event-window ATT visibility, but not an asymptotic IF-efficiency theorem.
pid_did_anticipation_bounded confirms BJS, SA, CS, and DCDH are already used as staggered-DiD anchors and flags no-anticipation as a separate partial-ID frontier.
No prior proposal directly proves or proposes a four-estimator BJS/SA/CS/Wooldridge influence-function efficiency boundary.
